content.text: Enterprise integrations connect Claude to the tools and data sources that organisations already use. Without integration, Claude is a general-purpose assistant that can answer questions but cannot act on or read from the systems where an organisation's actual work lives. With integration, Claude can summarise a customer's Salesforce history, review a GitHub pull request against team standards, or draft a Confluence page based on a Jira ticket — all within the same conversation.

There are four distinct integration paths, and choosing the right one depends on whether engineering resources are available, whether the workflow is human-driven or automated, and whether the target system is in a supported list or requires custom work.

attrs.alt: Enterprise integration architecture showing OAuth connectors on the left and API/MCP integrations on the right, both connecting to Claude

content.text: The architecture above shows the two main integration families: OAuth connectors (used via the Cowork interface without any engineering) on the left, and API or MCP-based integrations (requiring engineering) on the right. The choice between them is primarily a question of which systems need to be connected and who can build the integration.
OAuth Connectors (Cowork)

OAuth connectors are the zero-engineering path. They work within the Cowork product (Anthropic's agentic interface, available on Team and Enterprise plans) and grant Claude scoped access to a user's existing accounts in supported tools. The user authorises the connection once via an OAuth flow; Claude can then read from and act on that system within the scope the user granted.
Supported Connector Categories
Category
Example connectors
What Claude can do
Productivity suites
Google Workspace (Drive, Gmail, Docs, Calendar), Microsoft 365 (OneDrive, Outlook, Teams, SharePoint)
Read files, draft emails, create calendar events, summarise documents, search Drive or OneDrive
Communication
Slack
Read channel messages, post messages, search conversations, summarise threads
Developer tools
GitHub
Read repos, comment on PRs, create issues, summarise diffs, run searches across code
Project management
Jira, Linear, Asana, Notion
Read tickets, update status, create issues, summarise sprint state
Documentation
Confluence, Notion
Read pages, create new pages, update content, search spaces
Web browsing
Chrome connector (browse the web)
Fetch pages, extract content, research and summarise URLs
How OAuth Authorization Works

When a user adds a connector in Cowork, they go through a standard OAuth 2.0 flow: they are redirected to the third-party service (Google, Microsoft, GitHub, etc.), they approve the requested permissions, and the service issues an access token. Claude (via Cowork) then uses that token to make API calls on the user's behalf.

Scopes are set at connection time: the permissions granted cannot be expanded without re-authorising. If you connect Google Drive with read-only scope, Claude cannot write files until you re-authorise with write scope.
Tokens are per-user: each user connects their own accounts. There is no shared service account. A connector that accesses Alice's Google Drive only accesses Alice's files.
Revocation: users can revoke connector access at any time from the Cowork connector settings page. The third-party service's OAuth revocation page also works.
Retention: access tokens are stored by Anthropic for the duration of the connection. Anthropic's data handling terms cover these tokens under the Enterprise plan's DPA.
Workspace-Level Connector Policies

On Enterprise, workspace admins can control which connectors members are allowed to use. This is important when an organisation's security policy prohibits certain external data flows — for example, prohibiting the Slack connector from reading messages containing customer data, or restricting GitHub access to internal repositories only.
Admins can disable specific connector types for their workspace (e.g. disabling Gmail to prevent sensitive email data from being read by Claude).
Admins can require approval before a member can add a new connector connection.
Connector usage is logged to the audit trail under Enterprise: which user connected which service, when, and what scopes were granted.
MCP Server Integrations

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that allows any data source or tool to be exposed to Claude as a structured interface. Unlike OAuth connectors (which are built and maintained by Anthropic), MCP servers are built by the organisation connecting the system. This gives full flexibility: any system that has an API can be wrapped in an MCP server and made available to Claude.
MCP Architecture
An MCP server is a lightweight service that:
Exposes a set of tools (functions Claude can call) and resources (data Claude can read).
Handles authentication to the underlying system (API key, OAuth, service account).
Transforms the underlying system's response into a format Claude can consume.
Runs in your infrastructure — it is not hosted by Anthropic.

This MCP server exposes two tools to Claude: `get_customer` and `list_open_tickets`. When Claude is given access to this server, it can call these tools during a conversation to retrieve live CRM data without the user having to copy and paste anything.
Hosting an MCP Server

MCP servers run via stdio (piped to the Claude client) or as HTTP servers with Server-Sent Events (SSE) for remote access. For enterprise use, the SSE transport is standard — it allows the MCP server to run on your infrastructure and be accessed by Claude over HTTPS.
Docker container on internal Kubernetes cluster: the most common enterprise hosting pattern. The MCP server runs as a container with access to your internal network and credentials.
Lambda / Cloud Run function: appropriate for low-traffic connectors. Serverless reduces maintenance overhead but adds cold-start latency.
Managed MCP platforms: third-party services that host and manage MCP servers for common enterprise tools (Salesforce, SAP, ServiceNow). Reduces build burden but adds a vendor dependency.
Direct Anthropic API Integrations

The Anthropic API is the path for programmatic integrations where Claude is embedded in a larger automated system — not just used interactively. API integrations require engineering effort but provide complete control over the integration's behaviour, error handling, and data flow.
Common API Integration Patterns
Pattern
Description
Engineering effort
Async document processing
A queue-based system where documents (contracts, support tickets, reports) are processed by Claude in batches. Results are written to a database.
Medium — queue, worker, DB schema
Inline API call
A backend service calls the Anthropic API synchronously as part of a request handler. Claude's response is included in the service's response.
Low — add API call to existing handler
Automated review pipeline
CI/CD step that sends code, docs, or data to Claude for structured review. Results are parsed and acted upon (post PR comment, block deploy, etc.).
Medium — CI integration + output parsing
Chatbot / assistant
A front-end interface that routes user messages to Claude via the API, managing conversation history server-side.
High — full conversation state management
Claude as decision node
An agentic workflow where Claude evaluates a situation and returns a structured decision (approve/reject/escalate) that the system acts on.
Medium — schema enforcement + validation gate
API Key Management for Integrations
API keys for enterprise integrations should be treated with the same discipline as database credentials:
One key per integration, not one key for all integrations. This allows per-integration revocation without affecting other systems.
Store keys in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager) — never in environment variables on shared hosts or in code repositories.
Set up spend limits on the Anthropic Console for each key. A runaway loop or DDoS attack should not be able to generate uncapped API costs.
Rotate keys quarterly. Automate rotation using your secrets manager's rotation policy.
Monitor usage per key. Unexpected spikes in a key's usage are an early indicator of a runaway process or key leakage.
Choosing the Right Integration Approach

attrs.alt: Integration method decision guide comparing OAuth connectors, MCP servers, direct API, and no-code tools

content.text: The decision guide above covers the four main options. In practice, most enterprise deployments use a combination:
OAuth connectors (Cowork) for everyday human-in-the-loop work with supported tools (Google Workspace, Slack, GitHub, Jira).
MCP servers for internal tools or data sources not in the connector list.
Direct API for automated pipelines, batch processing, and embedded integrations in internal products.
No-code tools (Zapier, Make) for simple trigger-action workflows where engineering time is not available.
Security Considerations for Integrations
Every integration expands the surface through which data flows to Claude. Each connection requires its own security assessment.
Risk
Integration type
Mitigation
Over-privileged OAuth scope
OAuth connectors
Request minimal scopes; review scopes at connection time; document why each scope is needed
Credential leakage in MCP server
MCP servers
Secrets manager for credentials; no credentials in code; audit MCP server code before deployment
Data exfiltration via prompt injection
All types
Validate and sanitise data before passing to Claude; use structured output schemas; don't pass raw user-controlled strings as instructions
Uncapped API cost
Direct API
Set spend limits per API key; alert on spend spikes; implement rate limiting in your integration layer
Stale access tokens
OAuth connectors
Implement token refresh; revoke tokens for departed employees via SCIM deprovisioning
Practical Integration Walkthroughs
Walkthrough 1: GitHub PR Review via Anthropic API

This integration runs as a two-file GitHub Actions workflow that sends a pull request diff to Claude and posts a structured review comment. The diff is capped at 50,000 characters to stay within context limits. Claude returns JSON (not free text), making the comment formatter reliable.

name: Claude PR Review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Get diff
        id: diff
        run: |
          git diff origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD -- '*.py' '*.ts' \
            | head -c 50000 > diff.txt
          echo "size=$(wc -c < diff.txt)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Claude review
        if: steps.diff.outputs.size > 0
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: python3 review.py

      - name: Post comment
        if: steps.diff.outputs.size > 0
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        run: |
          gh pr comment ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --body-file comment.md

import anthropic, json

with open('diff.txt') as f:
    diff = f.read()

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
message = client.messages.create(
    model='claude-sonnet-4-6',
    max_tokens=2000,
    messages=[{
        'role': 'user',
        'content': (
            'Review this PR diff for: logic errors, missing error handling, '
            'security issues, style violations. '
            'Return JSON: {"summary": "...", "issues": '
            '[{"severity": "error|warning|info", "file": "...", '
            '"line": N, "description": "..."}]}\n\nDIFF:\n' + diff
        )
    }]
)

result = json.loads(message.content[0].text)

# Write GitHub comment markdown
lines = [f'## Claude Review\n\n{result["summary"]}\n']
for issue in result.get('issues', []):
    icon = {'error': '🔴', 'warning': '🟡', 'info': '🔵'}.get(issue['severity'], '⚪')
    lines.append(f'{icon} **{issue["file"]}:{issue.get("line","?")}** — {issue["description"]}')

with open('comment.md', 'w') as f:
    f.write('\n'.join(lines))

The JSON schema enforced in the prompt ('Return JSON: {"summary": ..., "issues": [...]}') is key. Without a schema, Claude may return markdown prose that is hard to parse. Structured output makes the downstream comment formatter deterministic.
Walkthrough 2: Jira Ticket Triage via MCP Server

This MCP server wraps the Jira REST API and exposes a `search_issues` tool. Once deployed behind your internal network and configured as a Claude client connection, members can ask 'show me all open P1 bugs assigned to me' and Claude calls the tool with the appropriate JQL automatically.

Deploy this as a Docker container on your internal Kubernetes cluster. The MCP server runs on port 8080 and serves the SSE transport at `/sse`. Configure the Claude client to connect to `http://jira-mcp-server.internal:8080/sse`. Members can then reference live Jira data in their conversations without copy-pasting ticket details.
Integration Governance

As the number of integrations grows, governance becomes important. Without it, the organisation ends up with unaudited connectors authorised by former employees, MCP servers running on stale credentials, and API keys nobody owns.
Integration Registry
Maintain a registry of all Claude integrations — a shared spreadsheet or internal wiki page with one row per integration:
Integration name and type (OAuth connector / MCP server / direct API / no-code)
Systems connected and what Claude can read or write through this integration
Owner: the team or engineer responsible for maintenance
Auth credentials: which key, service account, or OAuth app is used
Data sensitivity: what classes of data flow through this integration
Last reviewed date and next review date
Periodic Review Cadence
Quarterly: confirm each integration is still needed; rotate API keys; check for deprecated endpoints.
On employee departure: revoke OAuth connections the departing employee authorised; rotate any API keys they managed; update MCP server credentials.
After API changes upstream: monitor for HTTP 4xx/5xx errors from MCP servers and API integrations. Upstream API deprecations break integrations silently if not monitored.
After incidents: if an integration produces incorrect or sensitive outputs, review the full call chain — what data was passed to Claude, what was returned, and where validation was missing.

Integration debt compounds: a connector that worked six months ago may silently return empty results after an upstream API version change. Add lightweight monitoring (error rate alerts, response freshness checks) to every production integration.
Summary

Enterprise connectors and integrations span four approaches: OAuth connectors in Cowork for zero-engineering access to supported tools, MCP servers for custom data sources that require engineering, the direct Anthropic API for automated pipelines and embedded integrations, and no-code tools for simple trigger-action workflows. Most organisations use all four depending on the use case. The decision factors are engineering availability, whether the target system is in the supported connector list, and whether the workflow is human-driven or automated. Security discipline — minimal OAuth scopes, secrets management for API keys, and prompt injection awareness — applies across all integration types.
